title: User's Guide
text: get started, let’s simulate data. ’ll design simulation people larger belief effects (higher values τ\tau) smaller belief updates (lower values α\alpha).1 make plots look like ones paper.
code: # Simulate data for active control design set.seed(617) n <- 500  # True APE is 1 - this is what we want to recover tau <- runif(n) tau <- tau / mean(tau)  # Normalize so mean is 1  # Make learning rates negatively correlated with belief effects # People with high tau (big belief effects) have low alpha (small updates) alpha <- 1 / (tau^2 + 1) sigma2 <- 1 / (0.5 * tau + 1)  # Introduce endogeneity that affects both priors and outcomes V <- rnorm(n) U <- V + rnorm(n)  # Generate the experimental data dt <- data.table(     tau = tau,     alpha = alpha,     Z = runif(n) > 0.5  # Random treatment assignment )  dt[, signal := ifelse(Z, 1, -1)]  # High vs low signal dt[, prior := V + sqrt(sigma2) * rnorm(n) / 5] dt[, posterior := alpha * (signal - prior) + prior]  # Bayesian updating dt[, Y := tau * posterior + U]  # Outcome equation dt[, Y0 := tau * prior + U]  # Counterfactual outcome (in panel, this would be before treatment)  # for panel designs, use changes  dt[, dX := posterior - prior]  # Change in beliefs dt[, dY := Y - Y0]  # Change in outcomes

title: User's Guide
text: Panel designs observe beliefs actions people information provision. standard approach regress change outcomes change beliefs.2 settings, LLS estimator perfect drop-replacement doesn’t require additional assumptions. lls command estimate CAPE curve conditional belief updates, directly observe data. , post-estimation commands can used access CAPE curve (plot) unweighted average partial effect (APE) standard errors (print). Importantly, lls command expects receive data changes, panel two observations per person. syntax simple:  Sometimes, ’s useful visualize CAPE curve absolute value belief changes x-axis. case, makes attenuation bias apparent, shows effects vary size belief updates regardless direction. can abs.x = TRUE argument:  plot command returns ggplot object, able customize plot using standard ggplot2 commands.3 Finally, can get APE standard errors printing lls return object.
code: # Panel LLS using observed changes panel_lls <- panel.lls(   dat = dt,     dx = "dX",           # Observed belief change   dy = "dY",           # Observed outcome change   bootstrap = TRUE,   bootstrap.n = 200 )  # Visualize how effects vary with belief updating plot(panel_lls) +  # plot returns a ggplot object, so we can customize it labs(title = "Panel Design: Effects by Belief Update Size",      x = "Change in Beliefs",      y = "",      subtitle = "Conditional Average Partial Effect") # Visualize how effects vary with belief updating plot(panel_lls, abs.x= TRUE) +  labs(title = "Panel Design: Effects by Belief Update Size",      x = "Change in Beliefs (Absolute Value)",      y = "",      subtitle = "Conditional Average Partial Effect") print(panel_lls) ## Local Least Squares (LLS) Estimation ## ==================================== ##  ## Average Partial Effect (APE): ##   Estimate:   0.9817 ##   Std. Err:   0.0265 ##   t-value:   37.0202 ##   p-value:   <0.001 ##  ## Normal CI (95%): [ 0.9298,  1.0337] ## Percentile CI (95%): [ 0.9357,  1.0339] ##  ## Estimation Details: ##   Bandwidth:   0.0500 ##   Bootstrap reps: 200 ##   Observations: 500 ##   Support points: 500

title: User's Guide
text: Active control experiments compare people receive “high” vs. “low” signals. standard apparoach analyze experiments use assignment (example) high signal instrument posterior beliefs. lls estimator justified setting Bayesian updating assumption.4 see people update beliefs based signals, behavior “reveals” learning rates. , estimate CAPE curve conditional learning rates aggregate. Inferring Learning Rate Observed Update Bayesian updating implies posterior XX prior X0X_0 plus learning rate α\alpha times difference signal SS prior: X=X0+α(S−X0)X = X_0 + \alpha (S - X_0) Since know prior signal, can rearrange calculate α=X−X0S−X0\alpha = \frac{X - X_0}{S - X_0}. ’ve done , pass directly lls r argument; local regressions estimated condition variable pass r.  Let’s try taking detailed look CAPE curve. aggregated binned estimates estimation. makes easy change number bins, since don’t redo bootstrapping.  think first figure looks better! ’s bias-variance tradeoff – bins gives us detail, cost (much) wider standard errors. ’s expected, though, since ’re using less less data estimate effects bin. ’s important understand nbins option controls much “smooth” visualization CAPE curve; much smoothing done underlying local regressions (controlled bandwidth estimation option). Notice horizonal line (APE) figures; affected nbins (affected bandwidth).
code: # Calculate learning rates from observed updating dt[, alpha_est := (posterior - prior) / (signal - prior)]  # LLS for active control active_lls <- iv.lls(     dat = dt,     y = "Y",     x = "posterior",      r = "alpha_est",     control.fml = "prior",     bootstrap = TRUE )  # Plot conditional effects by learning rate plot(active_lls) +  labs(title = "Active Control: Effects by Learning Rate",      x = "Learning Rate Percentile",      y = "",      subtitle = "Conditional Average Partial Effect") # Plot conditional effects by learning rate plot(active_lls, nbins = 20) +  labs(title = "Active Control: Effects by Learning Rate",      x = "Learning Rate Percentile",      y = "",      subtitle = "Conditional Average Partial Effect")

title: User's Guide
text: Passive control experiments compare treated people “pure” control group receive new information. standard approach construct “exposure instrument” use instrument posterior beliefs.5 active control experiments, lls estimator justified setting Bayesian updating assumption. However, unlike active control experiments, control group researcher never observes update. case, need make additional assumption additional control variables can use predict learning rate. One potentially attractive option variance prior belief. Bayesian updating, learning rate proportional variance prior belief.6 example, assume know variance prior beliefs. See paper details various options necessary assumptions passive control designs.
code: # Create passive control data   dt_passive <- copy(dt) dt_passive[Z == 0, posterior := prior]  # Controls keep priors dt_passive[, Y_passive := tau * posterior + rnorm(n)]  # Case 1: Use prior precision to infer learning rates dt_passive[, prior_precision := sigma2]  # Higher tau → more precise priors  # Passive LLS (Case 1: observed prior variance) passive_lls <- iv.lls(   dat = dt_passive,     x = "posterior",              y = "Y_passive",              r = "prior_precision",   control.fml = "prior",   bootstrap = TRUE,   bootstrap.n = 200 )  # Plot conditional effects by learning rate plot(passive_lls) +    labs(title = "Passive Control: Effects by (Implied) Learning Rate",      x = "Learning Rate Percentile",      y = "",      subtitle = "Conditional Average Partial Effect")

what: Appendix: An Illustration of Attenuation Bias in TSLS and Panel Regressions
title: User's Guide
text: simulation, made learning rates (α) negatively correlated belief effects (τ). make standard approaches underestimate APE gives weight people update beliefs . true APE 1, expect TSLS small. can check running simple TSLS regression: expected, TSLS biased weights individual effects belief updating, people update (high α) tend smallest effects (low τ). simulation, TSLS estimate 0.757, significantly lower true APE 1. can also run panel (first-differences) regression. panel estimate 0.41 even biased downward TSLS estimate. use code lls package work, please cite working paper (pdf) Balla-Elliott, Dylan (2025). “Identifying Causal Effects Information Provision Experiments.” arXiv:2309.11387 information, visit dballaelliott.com.*
code: # TSLS: biased downward due to correlation between alpha and tau tsls <- feols(Y ~ 1 + prior | posterior ~ Z, data = dt) # Panel regression: also biased downward panel <- feols(dY ~ dX + prior, data = dt)

what: A Simple Example with Simulated Data
title: Quick Start
text: lls package comes simulated dataset info.sim highlight syntax works make example plots. plot function lls objects. returns ggplot object, can customize standard ggplot2 functions.
code: # Load the package library(lls) #> Warning: replacing previous import 'collapse::fdroplevels' by #> 'data.table::fdroplevels' when loading 'lls' #> Warning: replacing previous import 'collapse::fdim' by 'fixest::fdim' when #> loading 'lls' library(data.table) #>  #> Attaching package: 'data.table' #> The following object is masked from 'package:base': #>  #>     %notin%  # Load packaged simulated data data(info.sim) setDT(info.sim)  # ensure data.table  info.sim[, alpha_est := (posterior - prior) / (signal - prior)]  # Estimate using IV mode est <- iv.lls(info.sim, y = "Y", x = "posterior", r = "alpha_est",     bandwidth = 0.05, control.fml = "prior",     bootstrap = TRUE, bootstrap.n = 100) #> Bootstrapping with 100 iterations  # Print summary print(est) #> Local Least Squares (LLS) Estimation #> ==================================== #>  #> Average Partial Effect (APE): #>   Estimate:   0.9454 #>   Std. Err:   0.1046 #>   t-value:    9.0367 #>   p-value:   <0.001 #>  #> Normal CI (95%): [ 0.7404,  1.1505] #> Percentile CI (95%): [ 0.7385,  1.1404] #>  #> Estimation Details: #>   Bandwidth:   0.0500 #>   Bootstrap reps: 100 #>   Observations: 500 #>   Support points: 500 plot(est)

what: Local Least Squares Estimation for Information Provision Experiments
title: Local Least Squares Estimation for Information Provision Experiments
text: R package implements Local Least Squares (LLS) estimator identifying causal effects information provision experiments. LLS estimator consistently estimates Average Partial Effects (APE) even strong dependence belief updating belief effects.

title: Local Least Squares Estimation for Information Provision Experiments
text: can install lls GitHub using: Alternatively, can install source downloading repository:
code: # install.packages("devtools") devtools::install_github("dballaelliott/lls") # From the source folder install.packages("path/to/lls", repos = NULL, type = 'source')  # Or from a built tarball install.packages("path/to/lls_1.0.0.tar.gz", repos = NULL)

title: Local Least Squares Estimation for Information Provision Experiments

title: Local Least Squares Estimation for Information Provision Experiments
code: # Basic usage - Active/Passive Control result <- iv.lls(   dat = your_data,   y = "outcome_variable",    x = "posterior",   r = "learning_rate_variable",   control.fml = "~ prior + other_controls" )  # Panel Design result <- panel.lls(   dat = your_data,   dy = "outcome_change",   dx = "belief_change" )  # View results and plot summary(result) plot(result)

code: # Load the package library(lls) library(data.table)  # Load packaged simulated data data(info.sim) setDT(info.sim)  # ensure data.table  info.sim[, alpha_est := (posterior - prior) / (signal - prior)]  # Estimate using IV mode est <- iv.lls(info.sim, y = "Y", x = "posterior", r = "alpha_est",     bandwidth = 0.05, control.fml = "prior",     bootstrap = TRUE, bootstrap.n = 100) #> Bootstrapping with 100 iterations  # Print summary print(est) #> Local Least Squares (LLS) Estimation #> ==================================== #>  #> Average Partial Effect (APE): #>   Estimate:   0.9454 #>   Std. Err:   0.0912 #>   t-value:   10.3613 #>   p-value:   <0.001 #>  #> Normal CI (95%): [ 0.7666,  1.1243] #> Percentile CI (95%): [ 0.7853,  1.1509] #>  #> Estimation Details: #>   Bandwidth:   0.0500 #>   Bootstrap reps: 100 #>   Observations: 500 #>   Support points: 500 library(ggplot2) plot(est) +  labs(title = "Heterogeneous Effects of Beliefs by Update Size",      x = "Change in Beliefs",      y = "",      subtitle = "Conditional Average Partial Effect")

title: Simulated Information IV Dataset — info.sim
text: simulated dataset demonstrating information IV methods. Contains experimental data randomized information treatments Bayesian updating.

title: Simulated Information IV Dataset — info.sim
text: data.table 500 observations 9 variables: tau Individual-specific treatment effect parameters (normalized mean 1) alpha Learning rates, negatively correlated tau Z Binary treatment assignment (0/1) signal Information signal (1 high signal, -1 low signal) prior Prior beliefs (affected unobserved confounder V) posterior Posterior beliefs updating Y Outcome variable Y0 Counterfactual outcome (pre-treatment) dX Change beliefs (posterior - prior) dY Change outcomes (Y - Y0)

title: Simulated Information IV Dataset — info.sim
text: Simulated data

title: Simulated Information IV Dataset — info.sim
text: dataset generated endogeneity unobserved factors (V, U) affect beliefs outcomes. true average partial effect (APE) 1. People higher belief effects (tau) lower learning rates (alpha).

what: lls: Local Least Squares Estimation for Information Provision Experiments — lls-package
title: lls: Local Least Squares Estimation for Information Provision Experiments — lls-package
text: Implements Local Least Squares (LLS) estimator recover Average Partial Effects (APE) information provision experiments, robust dependence belief updating belief effects. Provides plotting helpers example data.

code: lls(...)  iv.lls(   dat,   y,   x,   r,   weights = NULL,   FE.fml = NULL,   control.fml = NULL,   bandwidth = NULL,   normalize.r = TRUE,   r.support.points = nrow(dat),   bootstrap = FALSE,   bootstrap.bayesian = TRUE,   bootstrap.n = 200,   ci.level = 0.95,   cluster = NULL,   n.cores = availableCores(omit = 2),   kernel = epanechnikov,   auto.trim = TRUE,   sd.trim = 0.02,   pointmass.zero = FALSE,   trim.zero = NULL )  panel.lls(   dat,   dy,   dx,   weights = NULL,   FE.fml = NULL,   control.fml = NULL,   bandwidth = NULL,   normalize.x = FALSE,   normalize.r = TRUE,   r.support.points = nrow(dat),   bootstrap = FALSE,   bootstrap.bayesian = TRUE,   bootstrap.n = 200,   ci.level = 0.95,   cluster = NULL,   n.cores = availableCores(omit = 2),   kernel = epanechnikov,   auto.trim = TRUE,   sd.trim = 0.02,   pointmass.zero = FALSE,   trim.zero = NULL )

title: Local Least Squares (LLS) Estimation — lls
text: ... Arguments passed lls.internal mode Character string specifying estimation mode: "iv" (default) "panel" dat data frame data.table containing analysis data y Character string specifying outcome variable name `dat` (IV mode) x Character string specifying treatment variable name `dat` (IV mode) r Optional; name variable `dat` giving ranks (IV mode) vector ranks directly pre-computed (default: NULL) weights Optional; character string specifying weights variable name `dat` (default: NULL) FE.fml Optional; formula string fixed effects specification (default: NULL) control.fml Optional; formula string control variables (default: NULL) bandwidth Numeric; bandwidth value, NULL use rule--thumb (default: NULL) normalize.r Logical; whether normalize running variable r equally spaced 0 1 within sign groups (default: TRUE). Ranks dense, tied values share rank spacing distinct values; group-level treatment, local windows contain uneven row counts (proportional group sizes). r.support.points Integer; number support points use estimation (default: nrow(dat)) bootstrap Logical; whether perform bootstrap inference (default: FALSE) bootstrap.bayesian Logical; whether use Bayesian bootstrap (TRUE) standard bootstrap (default: TRUE) bootstrap.n Integer; number bootstrap iterations (default: 200) ci.level Numeric; confidence level intervals 0 1 (default: 0.95) cluster Optional; character string specifying cluster variable clustered standard errors (default: NULL) n.cores Integer; number cores use parallel processing (default: availableCores(omit=2)) kernel Function; kernel function use (default: epanechnikov) auto.trim Logical; TRUE trim support points away zero avoid truncation. Sets trim.zero. (default: TRUE) sd.trim Numeric; trimming proportion SD calculation bootstrap (default: 0.02, 0 FALSE disables trimming) pointmass.zero Logical; TRUE, use near(x,0) instead kernel(x,0) (default: FALSE) trim.zero Numeric; threshold trimming support points away near zero values (default: NULL, uses bandwidth/2) dy Character string specifying outcome variable name `dat` (panel mode) dx Character string specifying treatment variable name `dat` (panel mode) normalize.x Logical; whether use ranks x instead raw values (default: FALSE; panel mode )

title: Plot LLS Results — plot.lls
